coredata.dc:title: Boosted photocatalytic antibiotic degradation performance of Cd0.5Zn0.5S/carbon dots/Bi2WO6 S-scheme heterojunction with carbon dots as the electron bridge
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Mingjie
Wang, Chunchun
Liu, Yanping
Yan, Ruyu
Li, Shijie
coredata.dc:description:
Building high-performance and robust S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts for sustainable pollutant abatement has been a prospective tactics to restore environment. Herein, Cd0.5Zn0.5S/carbon dots/Bi2WO6 (CZS/CDs/BWO) S-scheme heterojunctions have been designed and fabricated as an efficient and durable visible-light-responsive photocatalyst for tetracycline oxidation with 0.7-fold, 6.4-fold and 0.5-fold enhancement compared with neat CZS, BWO and CZS/BWO. The superb photo-activity is primarily derived from the synergy between carbon dots and S-scheme heterojunction, which not only effectively diminishes the interface resistance but also achieves the fostered disintegration and preservation of the photo-carriers with great redox ability. The impact of water conditions on the photo-activity is explored in view of the complexity of the authentic aquatic environment. The findings of the scavenging tests and ESR analysis evidence that h+,
O2 −, and
OH may mainly drive the antibiotic decomposition, mineralization and detoxification reaction. The photoreaction mechanism and TC degradation pathway over CZS/CDs/BWO are unraveled according to LC–MS analysis. The intermediate toxicity assessment indicates that most of the degradation byproducts’ acute and developmental toxicities are abated to a markedly lower level than that of TC. Moreover, CZS/CDs/BWO also could effectively degrade other popular pharmaceutical antibiotics (e.g., levofloxacin, norfloxacin, oxytetracycline, and enrofloxacin), demonstrating its universal photocatalytic potency for the treatment of pharmaceutical antibiotics. This work illustrates the potential practicability on carbon dots modulated S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts for efficient water remediation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel carbon dots mediated S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst of CZS/CDs/BWO was finely synthesized by simultaneously integrating S-scheme heterostructure construction and carbon dots decoration strategy and demonstrated highly efficient visible-light photocatalytic elimination of pharmaceutical antibiotics.

Building high-performance and robust S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts for sustainable pollutant abatement has been a prospective tactics to restore environment. Herein, Cd0.5Zn0.5S/carbon dots/Bi2WO6 (CZS/CDs/BWO) S-scheme heterojunctions have been designed and fabricated as an efficient and durable visible-light-responsive photocatalyst for tetracycline oxidation with 0.7-fold, 6.4-fold and 0.5-fold enhancement compared with neat CZS, BWO and CZS/BWO. The superb photo-activity is primarily derived from the synergy between carbon dots and S-scheme heterojunction, which not only effectively diminishes the interface resistance but also achieves the fostered disintegration and preservation of the photo-carriers with great redox ability. The impact of water conditions on the photo-activity is explored in view of the complexity of the authentic aquatic environment. The findings of the scavenging tests and ESR analysis evidence that h+,
O2 −, and
OH may mainly drive the antibiotic decomposition, mineralization and detoxification reaction. The photoreaction mechanism and TC degradation pathway over CZS/CDs/BWO are unraveled according to LC–MS analysis. The intermediate toxicity assessment indicates that most of the degradation byproducts’ acute and developmental toxicities are abated to a markedly lower level than that of TC. Moreover, CZS/CDs/BWO also could effectively degrade other popular pharmaceutical antibiotics (e.g., levofloxacin, norfloxacin, oxytetracycline, and enrofloxacin), demonstrating its universal photocatalytic potency for the treatment of pharmaceutical antibiotics. This work illustrates the potential practicability on carbon dots modulated S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts for efficient water remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the overconsumption of pharmaceutical and personal care products (PPCPs) along with the ever-increasing discharge of them into the aquatic environment has incurred a horrible environmental pollution. Thus, aquatic environment polluted by PPCPs urgently need to explore eco-friendly, sustainable, efficient and advanced water treatment techniques [1,2]. Solar-powered photocatalysis undoubtedly represents one of the most prospective water remediation techniques since it can not only effectively degrade the refractory contaminants but also mineralize them into nontoxic molecules such as CO2 and H2O [3–8]. To promote its industrial application, it is of paramount importance to create preeminent photocatalysts with remarkable visible-light response and catalytic performance, which is still a huge challenge [9–18].

Among visible-light-stimulated photocatalysts, Zn0.5Cd0.5S, one of significant CdS-based solid solutions, has emerged as a star photocatalyst for green energy production and pollutant elimination by virtue of low cost, high photo-activity, distinctive visible-light response and carrier mobility [19,20]. Noteworthily, Zn0.5Cd0.5S has the remarkable photo-reduction ability originating from its highly negative conduction band (CB) position, which potentially avails the photo-reduction reactions. Nonetheless, original Zn0.5Cd0.5S performs unsatisfactory catalytic performance in pollutant destruction on account of the severe electron–hole (e––h+) reintegration and weak oxidation potential [21].

On the one hand, building novel S-scheme heterojunction system has emerged as an attractive tactic for the advancement of photocatalytic efficacy of the photocatalysts [22–24]. Such unique S-scheme mechanism warrants the effective detachment of e––h+ as well as the great oxidation and reduction potential of the heterojunction, therefore promoting the photoreaction [24–30]. To take the full advantage of the redox potential of CZS, a suitable oxidation semiconductor should be chosen to scientifically integrate with CZS, which will be ideal once it meets the primary requirements including impressive visible-light absorption, intense interactions, and staggered energy alignment with CZS. Bi2WO6 (BWO), a powerful oxidation semiconductor, have demonstrated the preeminent potential in the photocatalysis field owing to its unique layer architecture, good chemical/physical stability, cost effectiveness, and favorable band structure (Eg ≈ 2.4–2.8 eV) [31–35]. More impressively, the matched band structures and pronouncedly varied work functions are conducive to the creation of CZS/BWO S-scheme heterojunction.

On the other hand, carbon dots (CDs), as a new class of carbon nanomaterials, have been utilized to combined with semiconductors to achieve the photo-activity upgrading since CDs could serve as the high-speed charge transfer channels as well as strengthen the sunlight utilization [36–41]. Therefore, simultaneously integrating S-scheme heterostructure construction and carbon dots decoration strategies is anticipated to obtain outstanding CZS/CDs/BWO heterojunction photocatalysts for effective removal of PPCP.

Enlightened by the above considerations, a novel carbon dots mediated S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst of CZS/CDs/BWO was finely designed and synthesized via a facile route. As anticipated, the optimal CZS/CDs/BWO (CZS/CDs/BWO–2) heterojunction demonstrates a remarkable TC degradation rate, far exceeding that of BWO, CZS, CDs/BWO and CZS/BWO. Furthermore, the unique charge transport mode in the S-scheme heterojunction with carbon dots as the electron bridge pronouncedly suppresses the photo-corrosion of CZS and the e––h+ reintegration, thus endowing the heterojunction photocatalyst with superior photocatalytic performance and durability.

The diagram of the fabrication procedure of the Cd0.5Zn0.5S/carbon dots/Bi2WO6 (CZS/CDs/BWO) heterojunctions is exhibited in Fig. S1
.

Preparation of carbon dots (CDs): The CDs were synthesized using the reported approach [42]. Urea (1.0 g) and citric acid (3.0 g) were sequentially dissolved into ultrapure water (10 mL) with sonicating and stirring treatment. The resultant solution was poured into a 50 mL autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After reaction, the suspension was first treated by centrifugation (10000 rpm, 30 min) to discard the bulk particles and then CDs were harvested by freeze-drying in a freeze dryer.

Preparation of Bi2WO6 (BWO): Firstly, 2 mmol (0.98 g) of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dispersed in 30 mL of HNO3 (0.4 mol/L) solution, followed by continuous agitation for 20 min. Meanwhile, 1 mmol (0.3299 g) of Na2WO4·5H2O was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water with stirring for 30 min to acquire the Na2WO4·5H2O solution (0.05 mol/L), which was added dropwise into Bi(NO3)3·5H2O solution and stirred for 24 h to get a homogeneous suspension. Afterwards, the reaction solution was poured into a Teflon-lined autoclave (volume: 100 mL) and then treated at 160 °C for 20 h. Subsequently, the product (BWO) was filtered, washed thoroughly using deionized water and ethanol for five times, and then dried in a vacuum drying oven at 80 °C overnight.

Preparation of Cd0.5Zn0.5S (CZS): Cd(CH3COO)2·2H2O (3 mmol, 0.8 g) and Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O (3 mmol, 0.6583 g) were respectively dissolved in 25 mL of ultrapure water, and then the above solutions were blended completely with 15 min of continuous stirring, followed by the adjustment of the pH of the solution to 7.3 utilizing NaOH solution (1 M). After that, 10 mL of 0.9 M Na2S·9H2O solution was dropwise added into the above mixture with continuous stirring for 18 h. The reaction solution put into autoclave went through hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 12 h. Last, the obtained CZS was washed and dried overnight.

Preparation of CZS/CDs/BWO heterojunctions: Briefly, a certain amount of CDs and 0.2 g of CZS were dispersed in 50 mL of ethanol until the formation of a homogeneous suspension with sonication for 30 min. Subsequently, 0.2 g of BWO was introduced into the above suspension with sonication for 5 min and stirring for 3 h. Finally, the obtained solids were dried overnight and then calcined at 120 °C for 1 h with under N2 atmosphere. Eventually, the collected CZS/CDs/BWO samples with different weight ratio (0.5, 1.0 and 3.0 wt%) of CDs were defined as CZS/CDs/BWO–1, –2 and –3.

The visible-light-initiated photo-decomposition of antibiotics including tetracycline (TC), levofloxacin (LEV), norfloxacin (NOR), oxytetracycline (OTC), and enrofloxacin (ENR) over the as-developed materials were implemented in a glass reactor. Prior to simulated sunlight illumination (300 W xenon lamp, λ ≥ 420 nm), the antibiotic solutions (20 mg/L, 60 mL) and materials (10 mg) were sufficiently blended by sonication for 30 s and agitation for 30 min to warrant the absorption saturation. The photo-reaction was triggered by turning on the lamp. The dispersions (1 mL) were withdrawn every 10 min and centrifuged to get the clear supernatant prior to analysis. The concentration variations of TC, LEV, NOR, OTC and ENR were monitored applying a UV–vis spectrophotometer at wavelength of 357, 290, 271, 354, and 271 nm, respectively. The cycle experiment of tetracycline (TC) elimination was performed and the procedures were as follows: After each run, the photocatalysts were recycled and washed for four times with deionized water, and dried at 60 °C overnight for next run. All photoreaction experiments were implemented in triplicate.

The crystalline phases of BWO, CZS/BWO, CZS, and CZS/CDs/BWO heterojunctions were scrutinized by X–ray diffraction (XRD) (Fig. 1
a). The diffraction pattern of BWO corresponds to orthorhombic Bi2WO6 (JCPDS 73-1126) [24]. The typical diffraction peaks of CZS are indexed to hexagonal Cd0.5Zn0.5S (JCPDS 89-2943) [43]. CZS/BWO and CZS/CDs/BWO heterojunctions exhibit the similar diffraction peaks, which match well with both BWO and CZS. Note that no signal ascribed to CDs in CZS/CDs/BWO heterojunctions was identified probably because of the tiny loading amount, high dispersion and poor crystallinity of CDs in the heterojunctions [44,45].

The existence of CDs in CZS/CDs/BWO–2 heterojunction can be verified by the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) measurements (Fig. 1 b). With respect to BWO, the peaks centered at 560 cm−1 and 730 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of Bi–O and W–O [44]. As for CDs, the peaks at ∼1195 cm−1 and 1550 cm−1 stem from to the C–O and C=C groups [44,45]. The peak at 1396 cm−1 is attributed to the telescopic vibration of the C–H, which is originated from the incompletely carbonized citric acid. Moreover, the absorption band around 1669 cm−1 is associated with the C=O of –COOH [45]. It is noted that the characteristic absorption bands of BWO and CDs can be identified in the CZS/CDs/BWO–2 spectrum, while those of CZS are not distinct, probably due to its weak absorption peak intensity. Evidently, the result substantiates the presence of CDs in CZS/CDs/BWO–2.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded to check the chemical states of elements in BWO, CZS, and CZS/CDs/BWO–2 (Fig. 1 c). The full-scan spectra uncloses that CZS/CDs/BWO–2 is primarily constituted by Cd, Zn, S, Bi, W, O, and C elements (Fig. 1 c). For CZS/CDs/BWO–2, the Cd 3d peaks at 404.85 and 411.60 eV belong to Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2 (Fig. 1 d) [46,47]. The Zn 2p located at 1021.60 and 1044.75 eV are assigned to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 (Fig. 1 e) [48]. The S 2p signals at 162.65 and 161.50 eV pertain to S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 (Fig. 1 f) [49–51]. The Bi 4f situated at 164.50 and 159.20 eV are related to Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 (Fig. 1 f). The W 4f at 36.60 and 34.50 eV correspond to W 4f 5/2 and W 4f7/2 (Fig. 1 g) [44]. The O 1s can be de-convoluted into three peaks centered at 529.50, 530.95 and 532.70 eV, which pertain to the lattice oxygen, the surface adhered oxygen and adhered H2O on sample (Fig. 1 h) [52]. The band energy (BE) of C 1s are located at 284.60, 285.52, 286.26 and 288.14 eV, belonging to the C=C, C–O, C=O and O=C–O bonds, respectively [45,53] (Fig. 1 i). Of note, the BE of Cd 3d, Zn 2p and S 2p in the CZS/CDs/BWO–2 shifts positively comparing with neat CZS, whereas, the BE of Bi 4f, W 4f and O 1s in the CZS/CDs/BWO–2 decreases compared to pristine BWO. XPS results signify that the formation of strong interactions between the components and the oriented electron transport pathway, giving rise to the establishment of an internal electric field (IEF) pointing from CZS to BWO, which is helpful to the effective spatial dissolution of the high-energy electrons/holes.

The micro-morphology of BWO and CZS/CDs/BWO–2 was scrutinized by SEM and TEM characterization. The SEM image in Fig. 2
a demonstrates that BWO has flower-shaped architecture constituted by nanosheets. The SEM images (Fig. 2 b-d) and TEM images (Fig. 2 e, f) witness the triumphant synthesis of the CZS/CDs/BWO heterostructure, which vividly uncloses that zero-dimensional (0D) CZS NPs (size: ∼8–40 nm) and 0D CDs (size: ∼3–8 nm) are deposited onto BWO microflowers with compact contact. Note that the well-designed heterostructure is helpful to the utilization of photo-carriers. Further HRTEM image (Fig. 2 g) identifies two kinds of lattice spacing of 0.31 and 0.27 nm belonging to the (101) plane of CZS (JCPDS 89-2943) and (002) facet of BWO (JCPDS 73-1126), respectively. The EDX analysis (Fig. 2 h) evidences the surface elemental composition (Cd, Zn, S, C, Bi, W, and O) of CZS/CDs/BWO–2. Further, the EDX mapping in Fig. 2 h reveals a uniform distribution of these constituents, indicating the closely and evenly wrapped CZS/CDs around BWO spheres.

Photo-degradation tests with TC as the model contaminant were implemented to appraise the visible-light-stimulated catalytic capabilities of as-built samples (Fig. 3
a). The blank experiment substantiates that nearly no decomposition of TC occurs in the absence of photocatalyst, unveiling that the photocatalyst is an indispensable component for TC photo-degradation. BWO and CZS could only decompose 33.8% and 52.9% of TC within 40 min, and the TC elimination rates are enhanced to 71.7% and 44.7% after the creation of CZS/BWO and CDs/BWO due to the boosted interfacial migration and separation of photo-carriers. Intriguingly, the ternary CZS/CDs/BWO heterojunctions achieve appreciably upgraded photo-activity (79.2% for CZS/CDs/BWO–1, 85.2% for CZS/CDs/BWO–2, and 82.0% for CZS/CDs/BWO–3), reflecting that the incorporation of CDs effectively advances the photo-activity of CZS/BWO, which is ascribed to that CDs not only optimize the sunlight harvesting behavior but also function as electron bridge that expedites the S-scheme photo-carrier detachment. Especially, CZS/CDs/BWO–2 has the most powerful photo-activity. Besides, the TC degradation performance of CZS/CDs/BWO–2 microspheres is comparable to those of other Bi2WO6-based photocatalysts, as listed in Table. S1. Noteworthily, excess CDs pose a negative effect on the photo-activity of the heterojunction, which could be explained by that the high content of CDs results in a competition for absorption of photons [45,53]. Remarkably, the apparent rate constant (k) value of CZS/CDs/BWO–2 (0.0398 min−1) is pronouncedly advanced for more than 0.7, 6.4 and 0.5 times to that of neat CZS (0.0148 min−1), BWO (0.0054 min−1) and CZS/BWO (0.0259 min−1) (Fig. 3 b). Above results certifies that the creation of ternary CZS/CDs/BWO heterojunction is effective in the advancement of photocatalytic performance.

The influences of key experimental parameters on TC photo-degradation performance in the CZS/CDs/BWO–2/visible-light system were assessed (Fig. 3 c-e). As observed in Fig. 3 c, with the CZS/CDs/BWO–2 amount augmenting from 5 to 50 mg, the elimination rate of TC is improved due to the increasing number of reactive sites [45,54]. Noticeably, the TC elimination rate by employing 10 mg of sample has up to 85.2%, which is not appreciably lower than that utilizing 50 mg (91.8%). From Fig. 3 d, Cl–, SO4 2–, HCO3 – or NO3 – ion exerts a negligible effect on the photo-activity of CZS/CDs/BWO–2 [45]. By contrast, the presence of PO4 3– notably impedes the elimination of TC, probably arising from the rapid capture of ROS by PO4 3– [45]. Furthermore, the TC photocatalytic elimination performance of CZS/CDs/BWO–2 was inspected in the presence of humic acid (HA) with different concentrations (5–20 mg/L) (Fig. 3 e). Clearly, TC degradation process is hampered by HA, which is related to the competition between the HA and CZS/CDs/BWO–2 for the ROS [24,55].

Beyond that, total organic carbon (TOC) analysis was executed to unveil the mineralization capacity of CZS/CDs/BWO–2 ( Fig. 3 f). It is distinct that about 8.2%, 18%, and 68.1% of TOC of TC solutions are removed by BWO, CZS and CZS/CDs/BWO–2 within 40 min, demonstrating the admirable mineralization capability of CZS/CDs/BWO–2.

The specific contributions made by photo-created reactive radicals to the decomposition of TC in the CZS/CDs/BWO–2/visible light system were clarified via trapping experiments (Fig. 3 h). Notably, the scavengers, namely isopropyl alcohol (IPA), 2, 2, 6, 6–tetramethylpiperidinooxy (TEMPO), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) hampers the TC decomposition reaction, rendering the removal rate to diminish from 85.2% to 58.7% (IPA), 67.6% (TEMPO), and 13.5% (EDTA-2Na) within 40 min. Evidently, photo-created h+ and
OH contribute principally to the photoreaction while
O2 – makes an auxiliary contribution.

To illustrate the photocatalytic performance against antibiotics comprehensively, the photo-degradation of other representative pharmaceutical antibiotics over CZS/CDs/BWO–2 was further implemented, including levofloxacin, norfloxacin, oxytetracycline, and enrofloxacin (Fig. 3 i). Impressively, the photo-degradation efficiencies of levofloxacin, norfloxacin, oxytetracycline, and enrofloxacin within 40 min by CZS/CDs/BWO-2 reach 73.9%, 53.7%, 81.6%, and 38.9%, respectively, evidencing the universal photo-activity of CZS/CDs/BWO–2. To evaluate whether CZS/CDs/BWO-2 could efficiently degrade the antibiotics simultaneously, the photo-degradation reaction in water containing several antibiotics (TC, LEV, NOR and OTC) was conducted ( Fig. S4 ). Clearly, CZS/CDs/BWO-2 can effectively degrade these antibiotics simultaneously. Above observations reveal the remarkable potential of CZS/CDs/BWO–2 for the purification of pharmaceuticals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Decomposition routes of TC and its eco-toxicity evolution
ce:section.ce:para:
To fully unclose the photo-oxidation process of TC over CZS/CDs/BWO–2, the HPLC-MS was utilized to determine yielded products ( Figure S5 ). On account of the detected intermediates (Table S2) and the reported works [56–61], three decomposition pathways of TC were speculated herein, as shown in Fig. 4
a. In Pathway I, the hydroxylation reaction and the subsequent conversion of enol to keto group triggered by
OH radicals lead to the formation of P1 (m/z = 427). P2 (m/z = 214) is originated from P1 via N–demethylation, deamidation, and ring-opening reaction. As the reaction proceeds, P2 is transformed into smaller molecules, including P3 (m/z = 158) and P4 (m/z = 101), through ring-opening processes and a series of dissociating functional groups (such as amino, oxhydryl and so on). In Pathway II, the C–N bond on the amide group is vulnerable to the attack by
OH radical, which drives the formation of P5 (m/z = 446). Subsequently, the group detachment reactions, (e.g., loss of N, N–dimethylamino, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups), as well as further ring-opening reactions give birth to P6 (m/z = 236) and P7 (m/z = 136). In Pathway III, TC is attacked by
OH and h+ radicals to produce P8 (m/z = 433) through hydroxylation and demethylation reactions. Subsequently, P8 (m/z = 433) go through a series of reactions to generate P9 (m/z = 349), including the oxidation of C–NH2 groups, dehydroxylation, deamidation and ring opening reactions. After that, P9 is converted into P10 (m/z = 305) and P11 (m/z = 261) due to the ring breaking and the removal of methyl and hydroxyl groups. Afterwards, a series of elimination reactions induce the creation of P12 (m/z = 136). Eventually, these intermediates would be mineralized into CO2, H2O and other smaller substances, as certified by the TOC findings (Fig. 4 f).

To clarify the bio-toxicity of intermediates, three key indexes (e.g., acute toxicity (Daphnia magna), developmental toxicity and mutagenicity) were determined via the Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T.) (Fig. 4 b-d and Table S3). From Fig. 4 b, the LC50 of TC for daphnia magna is 8.7 mg/L (defined as “toxic”). Distinctly, an increment of LC50 in almost all the products except P5 (5.35 mg/L) is found, especially P2 (351.25 mg/L) and P3 (166.05 mg/L) are deeded as “not harmful”. This fact signifies the lessened acute toxicity of the products. From Fig. 4 c, the photo-degradation of TC also achieves the effective reduction of the developmental toxicity of the TC intermediates except P1 (0.89) and P10 (0.95). Inspiringly, P7 (0.33) and P12 (0.41) are “developmental non-toxicant”. Fig. 4 d dispalys that mutagenicity of products excluding P1 (0.67), and P9 (0.57) is reduced compared with TC (defined as “mutagenic positive”), especially P2–4, P7 and P10–12 are “mutagenic negative”. All of these facts reflect the bio-toxicity of TC has been reduced after degradation reaction by CZS/CDs/BWO–2. In view of some toxic intermediates produced, longer reaction time to achieve the sufficient mineralization is required.

The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) measurements were executed to reveal the disintegration and transport efficiency of charge carriers. Distinctly, the arc diameter follows the sequence of CZS/CDs/BWO–2 > CZS/BWO > CZS > BWO (Fig. 5
a). Noticeably, CZS/CDs/BWO–2 has a substantially reduced diameter than other samples, reflecting the much weaker impedance encountered by photo-induced charges [10,41,67–72]. In the transient photocurrent response (TPR) curves (Fig. 5 b), the prominently stronger photocurrent-intensity on CZS/CDs/BWO–2 among all the as-fabricated samples evidences the reinforced separation and transit of photo-carriers, in agreement with the EIS finding (Fig. 5 a).

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra can be indicative of the e––h+ detachment rate in photocatalysts. The poorer PL emission intensity reflects more efficient disintegration of photo-carriers [48,65,73–79]. Of note, for CZS/BWO, a quenched PL signal is detected compared with BWO, signifying that the creation of CZS/BWO heterojunction avails the e–/h+ dissolution. Remarkably, after further introduction of CDs, the built CZS/CDs/BWO–2 delivers appreciably lower intensity than CZS/BWO, evidencing the effective transfer and dissolution of photo-carriers in CZS/CDs/BWO–2 (Fig. 5 c), which coincides with the results of TPR and EIS analyses (Fig. 5 a, b). Further time-resolved PL (TRPL) characterization reflects that CZS/CDs/BWO–2 owns an increased carrier lifetime (τavg: 1. 51 ns) in comparison with neat BWO (τavg: 1.44 ns) (Fig. 5 d), signifying the greater probability of more spatially separated photo-carriers involved in the photocatalytic reactions.

In view of the above analyses (Fig. 5 and
Fig S6 ), it can be rationally speculated that scientifically building CDs modulated S-scheme heterojunction of CZS/CDs/BWO could enormously increases the reactive sites, impedes the e––h+ reintegration and fosters reaction kinetics, thereby boosting the photo-activity.

The band alignments of BWO and CZS also support the creation of S-scheme heterojunction. First, the UV–Vis DRS measurements were executed to unravel the photo-absorption properties and bandgaps (Eg) of the samples (Fig. 6
a). BWO and CZS deliver the fundamental visible-light-absorption boundaries at ∼443 [33,52] and ∼537 nm [50,58], respectively. Apparently, the CZS/BWO heterojunction manifests the intermediate visible-light-absorption capacity between CZS and BWO. Intriguingly, CZS/CDs/BWO–2 presents a remarkably improved visible-light-absorption response compared to the counterparts, reflecting the integration of CDs effectively upgrades the sunlight harvesting ability. Meanwhile, the Eg of n-type BWO and n-type CZS were assessed via Kubelka–Munk function to be ∼2.75 [33] and ∼2.32 eV [43,50] (Fig. 6 b). Further M-S measurement uncloses that the flat-band potentials (Efb) of BWO and CZS are 0.18 and –0.93 (vs. Ag/AgCl), which are converted into 0.38 and –0.73 V (vs. NHE) (Fig. 6 c). In light of that the ECB of an n-type semiconductor is principally ∼0.1 more negative than its Efb, the ECB values determined are 0.28 V (vs. NHE) for BWO and –0.83 V (vs. NHE) for CZS, respectively. The EVB of BWO and CZS are 3.03 V and 1.49 V (vs. NHE) by Eg = EVB – ECB (1). Accordingly, the electronic band alignments of BWO and CZS are revealed and illustrated in Fig. 6 d.

ESR was acquired to better comprehend the photo-carrier transport mechanism. Fig. 6 e, f displays the DMPO–
O2 – and DMPO–
OH signals of BWO, CZS, and CZS/CDs/BWO-2 under visible light. BWO manifests no characteristic DMPO–
O2 – signal, primarily arising from the fact that its CB potential is not negative enough to reduce H2O/OH– to
O2 –. Besides, CZS exhibits no DMPO–
OH signal owing to its less positive VB edge. Intriguingly, CZS/CDs/BWO–2 obtains remarkably strengthened intensity of DMPO–
O2 – (DMPO–
OH) signal compared with CZS (BWO), implying more photo-carriers with remarkable redox potential are engaged in the creation of
O2 – (
OH) radicals (Fig. 6 e, f). These findings strongly substantiate that the CZS/CDs/BWO–2 belongs to an S-scheme heterostructure.

Accordingly, we speculate a possible mechanism of antibiotic degradation over CZS/CDs/BWO (Fig. 7 ). Specifically, once the formation of the S-scheme heterojunction with close contact, e– would promptly drift from CZS to BWO, therefore establishing an IEF directed from CZS to BWO and triggering band edge bending at the hetero-interface. When exposed to visible light, CZS and BWO can be stimulated to yield e– and h+. The IEF and curved bands would propel e– in the CB of BWO to recombine with h+ in the VB of CZS, while the highly reductive e– on the CB of CZS and oxidative h+ on the VB of BWO are well preserved and accumulated. The S-scheme photo-carrier transport route is helpful to the effective electron/hole disintegration as well as the conservation of the great thermodynamic-potential for producing ample ROS, including h+,
O2 –, and
OH species, which are engaged in the destruction of antibiotics[80 27,48,81–83]. It is noted that the CDs not only increases the specific surface area and ameliorate the sunlight absorption, but also function as a charge transportation highway, promoting the charge drift at the interface of CZS and BWO through an S-scheme mode [36,45]. To sum up, simultaneously integrating S-scheme heterostructure and carbon dots decoration is a promising tactics to achieve the improved photocatalytic performance. The antibiotic photo-degradation process over CZS/CDs/BWO can be well illustrated by the following equations.

A novel carbon dots mediated S-scheme heterostructure made of Cd0.5Zn0.5S nanodots/carbon dots/Bi2WO6 nanoflowers (CZS/CDs/BWO) was designed and developed. The well-designed ternary heterostructure unveils a boosting degradation performance towards pharmaceutical antibiotics, much higher than the counterparts. The prominent photo-activity stems from the significantly enhanced response to sunlight, increased specific surface areas, accelerated photo-carrier detachment efficiency, and strengthened photo-redox capacities because of the fabrication of favorable ternary S-scheme heterojunction. Moreover, the strategy of carbon dots mediated S-scheme heterostructure could effectively conquer the drawbacks of sulfide photo-corrosion, endowing CZS/CDs/BWO with excellent photo-stability. Furthermore, the effective mineralization and detoxification of TC antibiotic is also achieved under the attack of h+ and
OH and
O2 – radicals. This research provides new insights on exploration of carbon dots mediated S-scheme heterostructures as promising candidates for environmental protection.
